data.title: ManpowerGroup: Stabilizing, But Not Yet A Buy
data.description:
Introduction: Where I Stand After Q2

The general sentiment around ManpowerGroup Inc. (NYSE:MAN) over the last year or so has been decidedly bearish. On Seeking Alpha alone, I’ve seen 7 articles published in that period. 3 are Sell/Strong Sell, and the rest are Hold, with only 1 Buy position, and that came in nearly a year ago. The stock is down 40% in the same period, so I suppose it all tracks: investors look at market sentiment and the company’s trajectory and outlook, and markets follow investor commentary too, so it’s a bit of a vicious cycle.

Now, the company’s Q2 2025 results are in, and generally speaking, we should see a tiny bit of positive sentiment going off the numbers. Still, even though there aren’t any major surprises, I think the overall outlook is one of caution.

Still, the macro picture hasn’t changed much. Employers are cautious, client orders are choppy, and headcount adjustments continue to weigh on volumes. Structurally, the business is solid, and at these levels, a lot of bad news is already priced in, but I don’t see enough momentum yet to justify anything besides a Hold.

Company Overview

ManpowerGroup is a pretty simple company if we break down what it’s about. What it does is pretty straightforward: connect people with jobs. Most of its revenue comes from temporary and permanent staffing through its Manpower segment. Then we have Experis (IT and professional resourcing), Talent Solutions (RPO, MSP, and career transitions), and Right Management (leadership and outplacement services).

This is a global business with operations in 80+ countries, but Europe pulls the most weight, especially France. The country alone accounts for over 20% of MAN's total revenue. Needless to say, that kind of exposure comes with a lot of baggage: weak economies, tight labor laws, and fluctuating hiring cycles.

Now, management has been talking up their higher-margin solutions for years, and there’s real potential there. But the core business is still based on staffing, and when hiring slows down, as it has lately, we can see how quickly it hits the numbers.

I’m going to review Q2’s results below.

The Setup: Q2 2025 in Focus

Consolidated Revenue & Margins

Revenue came in at $4.5 billion, down about 3% year-over-year, and the weakness is most visible in Europe. The company’s EBITA (operating profit minus amortization & goodwill impairment) took a hit too, down 34%, and that reflects a tough combo of slower top-line growth, higher SG&A, and limited pricing power in a sluggish labor market. However, on the plus side, gross margins held relatively steady at 16.9%, so it’s not a total wash. If we go further down the P&L, we land at a GAAP EPS of -$1.44, dragged down by $88.7 million in goodwill impairments tied to Switzerland and the UK, plus some minor hits from the sale of South Africa and New Caledonia. Just strip that noise out, and we get an adjusted EPS of $0.78, which is above the company’s midpoint of guidance.

Regional Trends

The story has a bit more nuance when you break it down by geography.

  • Americas (23% of revenue): Revenue here was flat, but if adjusted for constant currency (CC), it rose 2%. OUP fell 20% (18% CC) to $36 million, with margin dropping 80 bps to 3.4%. The region is stable, but that stability is hiding some softness, especially in professional staffing and Experis demand. We can see that with the big decline from Canada, specifically.

  • Southern Europe (47% of revenue): Revenue rose 2% in real terms but fell 3% CC, while adjusted OUP dropped 16% to $75 million, with margin narrowing to 3.5%. This is MAN’s most dominant region in terms of revenue size and contribution, but we’re seeing it continue to be squeezed by macro uncertainty and competition.

  • Northern Europe (18% of revenue): Still a mess. Revenue dropped 10% CC and OUP margin slid into negative territory at -0.8%. I think the UK and Switzerland impairment charges point to structural issues here, and the performance is weighing on the entire company's results.

  • APME (12% of revenue): This was the bright spot. Revenue fell 8% CC, but operating profit margin improved 50bps to 5.1% as adjusted. The region is leaner, more disciplined, and generally more stable. Permanent hiring held up well, and we can see that the demand in places like Australia and Japan showed more life than their Western counterparts.

Now, I want to examine each business line’s performance.

Business Line Highlights

ManpowerGroup’s three core business lines told different stories.

  • Manpower Staffing saw 2% revenue growth (1% CC), and that marks a return to growth after several quarters of contraction. I’d say that the rebound is a positive sign, especially given the line’s heavy exposure to general staffing in a weak labor market.

  • Talent Solutions rose 3% reported and 1% CC. RPO trends were slightly better than Q1, and MSP continued its upward momentum. Right management was the main laggard here, and most of the pressure came from lower outplacement demand.

  • Experis is still the weakest link. Revenue fell 7% as reported and 9% in CC, dragged by the non-recurrence of healthcare tech projects in the U.S. It’s a tough reset for what was once a high-growth engine.

All things considered, then, this was a mixed quarter: far from a disaster, but it’s certainly not enough to flip sentiment just yet. I'd say that the headline numbers show that there's some stability. Still, I can't discount the margin erosion, regional underperformance (especially in Northern Europe), and sluggish demand in Experis because they all point to a setup that isn’t riding the challenges too well.

What I'm Watching in Q3 and Beyond

1. Experis Reset: Can It Stabilize?

Experis used to be Manpower’s growth engine, especially during the post-COVID IT boom, but that’s not the case these days. Q2 saw a 9% CC revenue decline, and we still don’t have real visibility into when the company will start seeing a rebound in demand for specialized tech staffing. I’ll be watching whether Experis can hold the line in Q3 or if the softness continues, because without Experis pulling its weight, there isn’t much of a near-term growth narrative.

2. Northern Europe: Structural Drag or Just a Rough Patch?

That -0.8% margin in Northern Europe is hard to ignore. Between the impairments in Switzerland and the U.K., ongoing macro pressure, and tight client budgets, this region is a real drag on MAN’s consolidated results. Management said they’re focused on fixing this via restructuring the business, and we can see some other signs of potential positive momentum. Still, any fix won’t happen fast. In Q3, I’m watching for how revenue stabilizes and whether the region can get back to breakeven margins.

3. Cash Flow & Cost Discipline

Additionally, it looks like Manpower’s cost control efforts are starting to pay off. SG&A as a percentage of gross profit improved slightly in Q2, and adjusted EBITA margin held steady despite the drag from Northern Europe. Q3 may be softer in terms of real numbers, but I’ll be watching free cash flow trends and how tightly they manage costs to protect profitability.

Valuation Check: The Market’s Not Wrong

Manpower’s stock has dropped about 40% over the past year, and it’s trading at $44.36 per share. That puts it at a forward P/E of roughly 18.2x, based on consensus estimates of about $2.95 earnings per share for FY 2025. The EV/EBITDA multiple is around 8x, and its dividend yield is about 3.34%, which is fairly attractive, but it’s partly a reflection of the depressed share price.

I’m interpreting all of those things, combined with the macroeconomic factors and the business’s performance in Q2 to mean that:

  • The market isn’t missing anything. Most investors are pricing ManpowerGroup as a cyclical, structurally challenged staffing provider.

  • That 18x earnings multiple suggests limited growth expectations, almost as if investors just want to wait it out.

  • An 8x EV/EBITDA is on the low end, even for staffing peers and outsourcing names (which tend to trade in the mid-teens).

Just looking at the multiples can be a tad misleading, though. After all, nearly everything is at a discount to MAN’s peers and its sector, so it’s likely that one might think it an undervalued opportunity right now.

The bottom line with the valuation as it is that there's little margin for error at these levels. The market’s clearly bearish, and it’s not unreasonable. If macro and staffing demand remain soft, then I think it’s more likely that the multiples will stay low or even fall further. Alternatively, if Q3 shows stabilization in Europe or signs of recovery in Experis, there’s a real shot investors could re-rate the stock. For now, though, it’s a Hold.

The Risks

  • Europe is fragile: Management says the softening in Southern and Northern Europe is stabilizing, but I’m not ready to take that as gospel. If the region gets any renewed macro weakness over the rest of the year, it could hit both revenue and margin pretty hard, and that tanks any positive sentiment Q2’s results may have built up.

  • Experis still has work to do: IT staffing is showing some signs of life, but what is more obvious is that the recovery is patchy. We need to see stable, consistent growth from this higher-margin segment; otherwise, that potential rebound will stay muted.

  • Valuation traps exist: Just because it’s cheap doesn’t mean it’s a bargain. I believe that if MAN’s EPS estimates fall or revenue keeps declining, then the stock may look deceptively “undervalued” for a long time.

Where I Stand Now

There’s a lot to like about how management is stabilizing things, especially with the margin beat and free cash flow this quarter. But the overall setup is still fragile, and the macro risks haven’t disappeared. The valuation isn’t demanding, but it’s not low enough to make me jump in, given all the moving parts. I’ll keep watching for consistent growth in Experis and margin recovery in Europe, but for now, I’m sticking with a Hold.

data.title: Gartner: Solid Fundamentals, Discounted Valuation
data.description:
I think Gartner (NYSE:IT) shares are attractive to buy right now. The company has a strong and sustainable model — annual research subscriptions provide stable cash flow and good margins. The securities are currently trading at a forward P/E of about 32x, which is 27% lower than the average over the past five years, despite the fact that both growth and business profile have remained at the same level. Active share buybacks help accelerate EPS growth, which is likely to support quotes. Altogether, a stable business, predictable FCF, and a reasonable valuation give me a reason to give a "Buy" rating. In this review, I will take a closer look at the key drivers and figures on which my thesis is based.

High-Retention Research Business Anchors Gartner's Cash Flows

Gartner, Inc. is a large publicly traded company providing analytical research, consulting services, and conferences in the field of information technology and business strategies. Most of Gartner's revenue (82% in 2024) comes from research subscriptions with a high proportion of renewals, while conferences and consulting account for 9-10% each and depend on one-time projects. Gartner operates globally: the company has over 2,500 analysts and 960 consultants in more than 30 countries, and products are sold in approximately 90 countries and territories.

Gartner's competitors include other large information services firms such as Forrester Research, IDC (International Data Corporation) and various independent consulting agencies, as well as analytics units in large IT companies (for example, IBM Consulting). In addition, open Internet resources and new AI services that partially meet the information needs of customers are becoming a competitive factor. Competition in the market remains high: according to the company, it faces many alternative sources of research and advice, and the barriers to entry are quite low.

The main demand for Gartner's services is based on the growth of IT budgets and business interest in new technologies like AI and the cloud - many simply cannot do without external expertise in these matters. The Gartner model positions the company favorably: thanks to long-term contracts and a high level of customer retention (multi-year contracts take up a large share), the company's revenue becomes fairly predictable. At the same time, the Conference business may vary (depending on the dates and format of events), but it is an important addition, especially as offline events normalize after the pandemic. As long as companies continue to invest in digital transformation and technology becomes more complex, interest in analytics and consulting is unlikely to weaken in the coming years. This creates the foundation for stable cash flow, which the market is still underestimating, especially against the background of adjustments in the tech consulting sector.

Q2 Set for In-Line Revenue, Mild EPS Upside on Buyback

In the first quarter of 2025, Gartner added 4.2% in revenue, which is $1.53 billion, and taking into account the currency, the growth is even higher, at 5.7%. All three segments have grown, but as before, subscriptions have made the main contribution: Research is once again pulling the main growth, while Conferences and Consulting are slowly recouping customers after a pause. Thus, the growth of Research sales and the delayed growth of the Conference provided sustainable operational results. Net profit remained at $210.9 million, while EPS increased to $2.71 (+1.5%), primarily due to buyback. In general, the profit was retained rather due to savings and careful handling of taxes.

Management has revised its expectations for the whole of 2025: revenue is now expected to be slightly lower — $6.535 billion against the previous $6.555 billion, but profitable metrics have been raised — Adjusted EBITDA is now $1.535 billion, and EPS is $11.70. The decrease in revenue is mainly due to the currency and current dynamics, while the profit growth is due to the same cost control, especially in the second half of the year, where the company promises to be flexible but careful with costs.

In the second quarter, I expect revenue to grow by about 5% year-on-year. Research will provide the main contribution again — customers continue to extend, retention is consistently high. In the Conference segment, demand is stabilizing and is likely to pick up due to the return of offline events and a seasonal revival. Consulting may add a little if partial project activity continues to recover. According to my estimates, the EBITDA margin will rise to 25.5%, primarily due to the scalability of the business and strict cost discipline. EPS is likely to grow slightly faster than profit: buyback continues to reduce the number of shares outstanding, so the range of $2.80–2.90 per quarter looks achievable. The consensus now expects EPS of $3.30 and revenue of $1.67 billion, with year-on-year growth of 2.6% and 5%, respectively. Overall, my expectations are consistent with the consensus, with a slight EPS upside due to buybacks and potential margin improvements.

Valuation Still Trails Fundamentals

I built the estimate on the DCF model, putting into it the unlevered FCF for 2025-2030. According to my calculations, Gartner's revenue will grow by an average of 9% per year, as I expect demand for analytics to recover, as well as the expansion of the company's product line during these years. According to my expectations, the operating margin will stabilize around 19% due to scalability and operational leverage. The capex will remain as low as ever and will be around 1.6–1.8% of revenue, while working capital will continue to steadily contribute about 2% to FCF. As a result, the company will steadily convert 20-21% of revenue into free cash flow.

I calculated the WACC at 9.03% (the prerequisites are listed in the table above).

If you look at the multiples, Gartner is currently trading at about 32x forward P/E and 18.3x EV/EBITDA. This is slightly higher than the average estimate for the IT sector - there are about 30.7x and 15.3x, respectively, but it is still noticeably lower than its own averages over the past five years: 44.3x and 22.9x. The same goes for Price/Sales — the current 4.3x level is higher than the sector average (3.1x), but inferior to the average of Gartner itself (4.8x). This arrangement shows that the market is still cautiously laying the potential, and future growth does not seem to be fully taken into account in the current price. If the company moves according to plan, there is a good chance that the multipliers will return closer to historical levels, and this will give the stock additional support.

In my DCF model, I take a forecast for free cash flow, calculate the WACC, and then go to the final estimate using the P/E and EV/EBITDA multipliers for terminal value. According to the scenario with a 9% profit growth and a P/E in the range of 26x–30x, a fair price is obtained in the range of $406–$434 per share, which gives an upside of 12-20% to the current price of $386. With an EV/EBITDA of 18x–20x, the estimate is slightly lower — $396–$430, with an upside of 9.5–19%. Both approaches show that stocks still look undervalued. Based on these data, we can talk about the underestimation of stocks relative to historical and market benchmarks, especially in the case of plans for profit growth. Based on consensus on EV/EBITDA 18x and P/E 28x, as well as a 9% increase in profit, the target price is $420, which gives an upside of about 8.8%. I assign a Buy rating with a target price of $420 with a 10-15% growth potential.

EPS Support Through Consistent Share Repurchases

Gartner historically does not pay dividends and focuses on the buyback program: for example, in 2024 it repurchased shares worth $735 million, and in the first three months of 2025 – another $163 million. This policy actively supports the share price and increases EPS by reducing the number of shares outstanding. At the same time, the company states that the return of capital through buyback remains a priority, while maintaining financial flexibility: there are no significant debt repayments planned for the foreseeable future, and free cash flows will be directed primarily to price support.

Risks

The deterioration of macroeconomics (lower IT budgets of companies, geopolitical instability) may slow down the growth of contract sales. Increasing competition: In addition to large consulting and media firms, Gartner is faced with free online solutions and rapidly developing AI services that may eventually take away some of its customers. Finally, internal risks include technological backwardness (the need to release in-demand solutions in a timely manner, including with AI functions), the difficulty of maintaining a qualified team of analysts and consultants, as well as operational risks (for example, failures at conferences or changes in tax and regulatory conditions). Any of these factors can negatively affect stock prices.

Final Thoughts

Gartner remains a fundamentally stable company with a predictable revenue model and high quality cash flow. The market assessment still does not fully reflect the growth potential, which creates a window for reassessment in the case of the medium-term plan. Given the current multipliers, DCF valuation, and capital return structure, the stock looks undervalued.

data.title: Fresh Del Monte Produce Remains Appealing As Q2 2025 Earnings Near
data.description:
Back in the middle of January of this year, I decided to reaffirm Fresh Del Monte Produce (FDP) as a ‘buy’ candidate. Even though the firm had experienced weakness leading up to that point, I argued that shares were cheap enough that recent improvements were attractive enough to justify a bullish outlook. Since then, the stock has risen by about 8.9%, outperforming the 5.1% increase achieved by the S&P 500. This comes at a time when revenue continues to drop. However, profitability for the company has ticked up, and shares still remain cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms.

Because of this, I am choosing to remain bullish. However, it is important to keep in mind that conditions can change as new data comes out. According to management, before the market opens on July 30th, the company will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of its 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts do anticipate that revenue will increase. But unlike what we saw in the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year, profitability is expected to pull back. So long as we don't see anything significantly outside of the norm in terms of volatility, I can't really see myself changing my mind. But if the data justifies it, I certainly will.

Shares Remain Appetizing

The most recent data that we have for Fresh Del Monte Produce as of this writing would be results covering the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. In the chart above, you can see revenue, profits, and cash flows, for this window of time compared to the same time last year. Revenue hasn't changed much. In fact, if you round it to the nearest $10 million figure, it remained virtually flat at $1.10 billion. This was in spite of the fact that the firm enjoyed a decent uptick from its largest segment, which would be its Fresh and Value-Added Products segment. Overall revenue grew from $676.8 million to $683.2 million.

That rise, according to the company, was thanks to higher per unit selling prices of avocados and higher sales volumes and per unit selling prices of fresh cut fruits in North America. Things would have been better had it not been for the fact that fresh cut vegetables and vegetables as a whole reported lower sales because of strategic operational reductions that the company implemented in the final quarter of last year. This included the sale of some of its assets from its Fresh Leaf Farms business.

The weakness for the company came from its Banana segment, which saw revenue contract from $379.5 million to $363.8 million. There was a slight drop from the Other Products and Services side of the company from $51.6 million to $51.4 million because of lower net sales of its third-party ocean freight services that it chalked up to weather related and port congestion impacts and the role that they played in reducing volumes. For the most part, it was the Banana segment that proved to be the problem. Based on the data provided, this was caused by lower sales volumes and per unit selling prices in Asia as demand decreased and as the industry experienced excess supply. Lower sales volumes in North America caused by industry supply and weather-related logistics disruptions also negatively affected results, as did certain foreign currency fluctuations.

Even though revenue for the company dipped ever so slightly, profitability actually improved. Net income went from $26.1 million to $31.1 million. This, according to management, was driven by a rise in the firm's gross profit margin from 7.43% to 8.39%, and it was also thanks to a reduction in selling, general, and administrative costs from 4.58% of sales to 4.38%. The picture here would have been even better had it not been for an extra $14 million gain on the disposal of property, plant, and equipment, that the company booked in the first quarter of 2024. So if anything, the improvements on the bottom line for the first quarter of this year were rather impressive.

For the gross profit side of things, it appears as though the company benefited from margin improvement associated with higher selling prices for pineapples and melons, as well as because of higher per unit selling prices in its poultry and meats business. Cash flows for the company improved also. Operating cash flow more than doubled from $18.7 million to $46.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $35.7 million to $46.3 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company grew from $43.5 million to $61.3 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2023 to 2024 fiscal years. Revenue declined, and bottom-line results were mixed. We do know that the big problem here ended up being its Banana segment, which reported a plunge in revenue from $1.64 billion to $1.48 billion. Management blamed this on competitive market pressures in North America, as well as decreased supply in the Philippines that negatively affected sales volumes in Asia. The latter of these seems to have been the result of weather-related events. Fortunately, the Fresh and Value-Added Products category for the company actually reported a nice jump in revenue from $2.48 billion to $2.61 billion. This was because of higher per unit selling prices and greater sales volumes in avocados and pineapples. Higher sales volumes of melons also helped.

Despite these mixed results, the fundamental picture for the company remains solid. The firm has net debt of only $204.5 million, which is small compared to its market capitalization of $1.59 billion and the fact that adjusted operating cash flow last year was $187.7 million. This suggests that the firm could pay off most, if not all, of its debt in a rather short window of time, even with factoring in capital expenditures like the $51.7 million it spent in 2024.

On top of this, the company remains attractively priced. Even if we ignore the recent increase in profits and cash flows that we saw in the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year, the stock remains cheap. In the chart above, you can see how it's priced using results from the last two completed fiscal years. These are low multiples in my book. Furthermore, they are also low compared to other similar companies, as shown in the table below. Using each of the two valuation metrics, we can see that only one of the five companies is cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Fresh Del Monte Produce 8.5 7.7
Limoneira (LMNR) 9.9 41.2
Village Farms International (VFF) 35.9 77.0
Mission Produce (AVO) 12.5 9.3
Calavo Growers (CVGW) 14.0 14.2
Dole plc (DOLE) 6.0 5.6

Of course, if management hits its targets for this year, things could look even better. Management is currently working on cutting costs and growing by means of acquisition and product innovation for the 2025 through 2027 fiscal years. In fact, the business recently purchased a Ugandan avocado producer by the name of Avolio and is targeting 2% sales growth this year as well as double digit gross margins for 2025. Considering that the gross profit margin for the company last year was 8.36%, that would be quite an increase.

Naturally, the picture can always change as new data becomes available. In the table below, you can see that analysts are anticipating revenue of $1.16 billion for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year that management will be reporting the results of on July 30th before the market opens. That would represent an increase over the $1.14 billion reported the same time last year. However, earnings per share are forecast to decline from $1.12 to just $0.95, taking net income down from $53.6 million to $45.9 million. If profitability does decline, it's likely that the other cash flow metrics will also drop year over year. But so long as the picture ends up not being worse than last year on a forward basis, I think investors have reasons to be very optimistic.

Takeaway

At this time, I think that the market is underestimating Fresh Del Monte Produce. Yes, the firm has experienced some volatility in the last couple of years now. But the good news is that shares are cheap, debt is low, and management seems optimistic when it comes to 2025. When you add all of this together, I believe that it makes for an appetizing prospect as earnings near.

data.title: Credicorp: Fundamentals Are Shining But Valuation Leaves Little Room For Upside
data.description:
Introduction

Credicorp (BAP) is a leading bank in Peru, with its operations extending into other Latin American countries and the US. The company has been going through a cleanup phase marked by a consistent improvement in the asset quality ratios. While this cleanup phase led to slow loan growth for the last few quarters, the loan growth going forward is expected to improve as management focuses back on growth after the cleanup. The NIMs are also expected to remain stable with repricing crosswinds from assets and liabilities. While fundamentals look bright, the company’s valuations do not have good upside potential. This, when combined with the political risk in Peru, discourages me from issuing a buy rating on the stock despite good fundamentals. Hence, I have a neutral (hold) rating on the stock.

Fundamentals Are Strong And Improving

Back in May, BAP came out with a robust start of 2025, with the company reporting significant YoY improvement in both the top and bottom lines. The topline came in at 1.44 billion, 7.4% higher than the same period last year, primarily driven by better NII growth driven by lower funding costs as well as growth in the non-interest income. On the bottom line front, the company reported $484 million in net income, growing 20% YoY.

On a more granular level, the company’s net interest income grew by ~4.3% YoY, primarily driven by lower funding costs due to the repricing of liabilities on the back of lower rates as well as a mix shift toward lower-yielding liabilities. During the latest quarter, the company reported a staggering 17% growth in the low-cost deposit balance, leading to an increasing share of this low-cost deposit, which is also evident in the consistently declining cost of funding over the last few quarters. As far as the yields are concerned, a similar dynamic is at play, but here the assets are being repriced, leading to declining NIMs.

With respect to loan growth, the company reported somewhat muted growth of ~1.5% on a YoY basis. This growth rate is in continuation from the last few quarters, where the company constantly posted low single-digit to slightly negative growth rates. The slower-than-expected loan growth is primarily attributed to a deliberate attempt to improve asset quality and tighten lending standards. Lending institutions like BAP have two options: either grow slowly with stable asset quality or grow fast and run a risk of asset quality deterioration. The management of BAP chose to grow slowly at better asset quality, especially when their main market (Peru) has been struggling with economic woes recently in late 2023, which is now recovering. Moreover, the company did a phenomenal job, with the company reporting 16% YoY growth in deposits, continuing the growth rate from the past few quarters. While the growth in deposits was partially fueled by the BCP Bolivia’s accounting adjustment, even excluding this benefit, the deposit growth would have been a robust 9.7% YoY for 1Q25.

With respect to asset quality, BAP has shown quite a remarkable improvement over the last few quarters. The company reported a YoY improvement of 112 bps in nonperforming loans (NPL); on a QoQ basis, the improvement was 16 bps. If we look at the overall trend for the last few quarters, there has been a steady improvement in the NPL ratio as well as other asset quality metrics. Going forward, BAP is expected to witness stability in NIMs as opposed to sequential decline from the last few quarters. The management has guided for a NIMs range of 6.2-6.5%, driven by the crosswind of asset and liability repricing. Moreover, a continued higher-margin mix shift toward lower cost of funding as well as a focus on the higher-margin retail loan segment should help boost underlying NIMs and partially negate the headwinds.

On the loan growth front, management has been guiding for a loan growth of ~3.5% for 2025 despite a slow start to the year at 1.5%. I believe the loan growth should pick up now, as the Peruvian economy has now recovered quite considerably. Additionally, BAP’s asset quality has shown quite a lot of improvement over the last few quarters, which should now at least make management a little less conservative. While there is still room for asset quality improvement, I believe it will continue in tandem with a little boost to loan growth as indicated by management’s slow growth guidance for 2025.

With respect to asset quality, the company has shown good performance over the last few quarters. I believe this improvement should normalize now, as management would steadily shift focus to growth now. Moreover, the origination of higher-risk retail loans should also lead to a headwind on the asset quality front. However, I believe this is quite normal for a company that is trying to pursue growth.

In conclusion, I believe BAP has come out of a long cleanup phase with low loan growth; now the loan growth is expected to pick up with normalization in asset quality improvement. Moreover, the Peruvian economy is faring well of late, and central bank rates are also quite conducive for loan growth. On the NIMs front, I believe the ratio could remain range-bound given the repricing crosswinds.

Valuation

BAP is currently trading at a 9.9x forward non-GAAP PE ratio, which is a notch lower than the sector median of 11.2x but significantly lower than its 5-year average of ~15.15x. According to consensus estimates, the company is assigned a growth rate of 9-10% for 2026 and 2027 which is a notch higher than its historical net income growth rate of ~6% (2015-2024). If we assume a forward growth rate of 7-8%, the PEG ratio comes under 1.1-1.3x, which is neither expensive nor cheap.

The forward price-to-book [P/B] ratio of BAP is currently near 1.8x, considerably higher than the sector median of 1.2x as well as its 5-year average of ~1.45x. However, when comparing the higher P/B ratio with ROE the company does not look expensive on the P/B valuation approach. The company has an ROE of ~16.55%, considerably higher than the sector median of 10.2% and its own 5-year average of 13.1%. The P/B of 1.8x and ROE of 16.5x give us a yield (ROE/price to book) of 9.2%, which is also neither expensive nor cheap.

Risk to consider

  • Rate cut risk: While the management has guided for a stable NIMs going forward, this estimation could have a downside if the Peruvian central bank cuts rates more than expected. While the central bank is not expected to cut aggressively going forward, a sharp change in global rates could pressure the central bank to cut more than expected.

  • Political instability: Peru has been going through political instability for quite a while with frequent changes in the government. While the company at times has mentioned that the country might be going through political struggles, it has managed to grow well. However, I believe it is still a big headwind for growth and exposes the company to major policy shifts.

Conclusion

While BAP’s fundamentals look impressive with improving asset quality and potential improvement in loan growth, the valuation does not leave much room for upside. Moreover, given the risk that the company has, especially political instability in Peru, it makes me even less interested in the stock. While this could be a good investment, I don't believe the current valuation justifies the potential risks the company has. Hence, I have a neutral (hold) rating on the stock.

data.title: Centerra Gold: Turkish Mine's Clock Is Ticking, But High Transition Potential Remains
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Centerra Gold (CGAU) - a smaller-size gold miner expected to produce 270,000 to 310,000 ounces this year - had quite a bad year in 2024 after no processing at the mine in Türkiye and lower grades in Canada. But, CGAU still managed to make quite a lot of money, and now, the company is actively looking into diversifying its production away from their main mines, as I will detail below.

Currently, their gold production is pretty much split between their Mount Milligan mine in Canada and Oksut in Turkey, with the first also producing ~60 Mlbs of copper and 13 to 15 million pounds of molybdenum on top. Now that their issues seem to have passed and production is recovering to their expected levels, their 2025 guidance points towards ~290,000 ounces of gold this year, with more projects in the pipelines paving the way for future growth ahead.

Despite the recent jump in CAPEX compared to 2021 (I’ll detail the significant impairments this company went through that led to back in 2020), they can still make significant cash flows. I’d still wait for more earnings reports to get a better idea about their potential in this regard. Still, based on the 2025 expected demand combined with their CAPEX estimates and the current price of gold, we could be looking at ~$329.4 million in operating cash flows near their midpoint. That’s the result of a ~21% decrease from production vs last year and ~39% increase from the current gold price vs 2024 averages. Plus, they expect to save ~$50 million from lower CAPEX. So, we’re looking at ~$220 million in cash flows in 2025, if gold prices remain around the current levels for the whole year. Based on their ~$1.5 billion market cap, that would place them at a P/FCF ratio of ~6.8, which is very good for gold miners in this environment.

Financially, based on their latest earnings report, we see an excellent position, with the cash and cash equivalents alone nearly covering ALL the liabilities. Not only that, but the company literally has zero debt, so that in itself is an opportunity, as they are in a position to issue quite a bit and boost their growth pipeline even above their estimates.

There is $1.66 billion in equity on a $1.5 billion market cap, despite the stock being up ~25% year to date. And keep in mind that the value of their assets is likely higher with gold at record-breaking prices, as their return would be higher. So, if they were to sell a mine that was valued at, let’s say, $200 million with gold at $2,500/oz, its value would most likely be significantly higher nowadays, with gold at even more than $3,400/oz, with potential for more, as I mention below. So, instead of a price-to-book ratio of 0.9 today - which is already great - we could be looking at even better levels in reality (using the net asset value, as we’ll see in the peer comparison segment), which could be a good margin of safety.

Dividends & Buybacks

On top of a decent ~2.8% dividend, Centerra Gold also does significant buybacks. Currently, they have an up to $75 million buyback program in place, meaning a yield of around 5%, for a combined total of 7.8%. This represents about $117 million, which should be a very sustainable yield of a bit more than 50% this year, if everything goes according to the estimates mentioned above.

Risks & Opportunities

From the start, I’ll note that the main risk here is that they currently depend on 2 mines. Any issue in either of them can decimate the company’s production. They actually went through a nationalization a couple of years ago, which cut the production in half, but more about that below. They even expect the Turkish mine to decrease significantly starting next year as the production cost would increase, meaning that they urgently need a replacement. There are a couple of mines they are working at, but that’s 3-5 years into the future. What they can do is look for a merger or some more acquisitions, because otherwise there isn’t that much they can do in the short term to avoid a significant production drop.

This could bring an opportunity and a quick way of making a profit from this valuation, as their North American projects could probably lead to a nice premium. But, they are well aware of this, and are currently on a spree of buying stakes in smaller miners, with 3 announced in the past year, on top of buying their own shares and the Nevada gold mine in 2022 - among others. So, Centerra is currently in a transition with a clear strategic plan of diversifying away from Turkey while squeezing everything they can from it, and potentially switching to a high-quality portfolio of mines in North America. Still, this exposes them to execution risk, especially since they would likely pay significant prices for these mines in this environment.

However, I also have to mention that back in 2020 (and definitively in 2022 after a settlement), they lost control of their biggest mine, following the decision of the Kyrgyz government to nationalize the 550,000-600,000 ounce mine (which makes it one of the biggest gold mines on the planet). This goes to show what “risky jurisdictions” refer to, and it’s a risk to always keep in mind with companies that operate there. We’ve seen this risk materialize countless times before, and even recently with B2Gold (BTG) - which I believe is a Strong Buy now - and Barrick (B) in Mali, after the country changed its mining code following the “coup within a coup” that led to the current president’s term. Of course, there’s a lot of uncertainty there, and a “risky” country could always decide to seize a mine (see Centerra), which could lead to significant impairments, especially since many of the miners that operate in these regions don’t have a very diversified portfolio of mines, and usually rely on 2-3 projects.

Still, today, Centerra offers exposure to Canada and Turkey, which is an interesting mix of risk and high-quality/low-cost assets, and they also bought the Goldfield project in Nevada back in 2022 as a response to their changing situation. But, Turkey is not the safest region [see Alamos (AGI)’s case, which I also wrote about recently], and they predict a sharp drop in production between 2024 and 2028 of their main gold mine located in Turkey and consequently a large increase in AISC. This could be one reason behind the lower PB, as the “leftover” Turkish assets would likely not be valued that high after 2029. I believe they could even look into selling the Turkish mine in advance and use the cash inflow to buy another mine - maybe through an acquisition (or more) of another junior Canadian miner, especially since they have over $1 billion in liquidity thanks to their cash on the balance sheet and ~$400 million undrawn credit facility.

But, remember that at the end of the day, the stock will depend on the price of gold first and foremost. Higher gold prices are very likely to attract higher stock prices, as the market will naturally expect better numbers from the company. On the other hand, lower prices are also likely to have the opposite effect, pushing gold miners into tougher times, making it harder for them to manage the debt, and perhaps even leading to mines closing or at least lowering their output.

I remain bullish on gold despite the significant jump we've seen in recent years, as there are several key catalysts that can lead to better prices. For example, rate cuts are probably the main near-term catalyst to keep an eye out for, and I believe they should come in the next couple of years regardless of what Powell decides. That is because his term ends in May 2026, and Trump is very likely to name someone who will obey him. Since he kept criticizing Powell for not cutting rates recently (despite saying he cut them too fast last year), we are likely to see him push his next proposed Fed chair into faster rate cuts. What it then does is weaken the dollar, which is great for assets such as gold, while also potentially making the market fear a harsh landing and even a recession. And that would probably come soon after the tariff situation becomes clearer, so the market would not have that much time to react to "calm" times and reevaluate its need for gold.

This is also possible because of the sources of demand for gold. As I mentioned in previous articles, gold's demand makes it a unique commodity, allowing it to respond differently to economic factors compared to other commodities. Thanks to that, we can even see fear of missing out, hype (which I think is more and more visible now), or other market phenomenon that can boost the price of gold even further, as it doesn’t depend on real uses - mostly jewelry (inelastic demand; won’t go down significantly if gold jumps even more now), investing, and banks. Especially if the market is then pushed into fear of a recession (or even a real one), we can see that it sustains higher and higher prices for longer.

Another point to remember is that the current AISC is much higher than back in 2016, for example, when it reached even less than $900/oz. That sustained the lower prices of ~$1,200 we saw back then, as a majority of mines were profitable even at that level. Nowadays, when the median AISC is close to $1,500, we can probably expect to see significant issues for miners if gold ever falls near that level again, as such prices would not be sustainable for the current state of the mining industry. This would then limit the supply and affect gold companies, potentially leading to higher prices - which is part of the cyclicality of the commodity.

On the other hand, Centerra's exposure to copper offers a long-term opportunity, and that is probably one of the metals I am most bullish on. That’s because of its use in electricity, which is expected to grow at a significant CAGR of even more than 6.5% from 2025 to 2030. A key factor to that will be electric vehicles, but also data centers, both of which will require very significant amounts of energy, meaning an accentuated need for copper. In a recent study called "The Future of Copper", S&P Global looked into two potential scenarios based on green targets. In both of them, they saw a significant supply gap in the early and mid 2030s that should have great effects on the price of copper. Still, that can also lead to alternatives such as carbon nanotubes, so there is potential to see some disruption here that can turn that supply gap into nothing, potentially even leading to an oversupply, which would be terrible for the price.

Peer Comparison

As they show in their peer comparison [compared to Lundin Gold (OTCQX:LUGDF), Alamos (AGI), Eldorado Gold (EGO), IAMGOLD (IAG), B2Gold (BTG), Equinox Gold (EQX) and others], Centerra comes at quite a significant discount to their net asset value. They are currently at a ratio of 0.6, which takes into account the current value of their projects, unlike the equity (which is backward-looking), compared to the peer average of ~0.9, indicating a big difference compared to their market cap.

Also, I’d like to note their excellent balance sheet once again, even when compared to bigger peers. I believe this $0.6 billion in net cash is definitely a huge advantage, especially for a market cap of only $1.5 billion, opening significant potential for mergers and acquisitions ahead.

Valuation

As for a DCF valuation for CGAU, I’ll start with a free cash flow of $220 million (the rough estimation for 2025’s level), and assume a growth rate of ~6% in the first 5 years as they replace their Turkish mine (backed by the >$1 billion in liquidity), followed by a 4.5% CAGR for the remaining period until year 10 and a 3% terminal growth rate, plus a WACC of 8.5% - in line with market estimates. With $600 million in net cash and 207.56 million shares outstanding, the model returns an enterprise value of ~$4.94 billion, which results in an equity value of ~$5.54 billion, implying an intrinsic value per share of ~$26.70.

This is nearly four times their current stock price, which is very significant, and might be worth the risk. In fact, I’d say that Centerra Gold, together with B2Gold, which I recently analyzed as well, are currently my only 2 strong buys in the gold mining industry, and I am looking forward to building a position in Centerra as well.

Conclusions

Overall, Centerra is definitely in a very interesting spot. With an excellent balance sheet and two mines that could provide significant cash flows even after the recent production drop, the company has potential to develop significantly, making me call this a strong buy now. Even when using estimates that would place them below what other junior miners plan to grow at, they are well below what I’d consider a fair value in this environment. Still, the risk remains, especially if the Turkish government becomes an issue, and the mine is on the verge of becoming obsolete unless there are some significant new discoveries. This puts CGAU in a spot where they will depend on the management’s execution, as their next steps will decide the company’s future.

data.title: Weekly Commentary: Not Restrictive
data.description:
In his argument for a July rate cut, Fed Governor Christopher Waller states that "tariffs are one-off increases in the price level and do not cause inflation beyond a temporary surge." Moreover, "a host of data argues that monetary policy should be close to neutral, not restrictive." "The [GDP and employment] data imply the policy rate should be around neutral, which the median of FOMC participants estimates is 3%, and not where we are-1.25 to 1.50 percentage points above 3%."

The Atlanta Fed GDPNow Forecast has Q3 growth up around 2.4%, a notable acceleration from the first half. As for the labor market, June's Unemployment Rate dipped a tenth to a historically low 4.1% (hasn't been lower since January). Data suggests labor market tightening. Weekly Jobless Claims dropped last week to a three-month low of 221,000. The most recent (May) JOLTS data surprised with a hefty (almost 400k) jump in job openings to a six-month high of 7.769 million.

July 14 - Bloomberg (Rachel Graf): "Companies rushed to tap the US leveraged-loan market for debt on Monday, leading to the market's busiest day since January. The week kicked off with 19 leveraged-loan launches, worth about $24 billion… That's the most since Jan. 21, when more than 30 firms tapped the market for $48 billion of debt."

More evidence this week that notably loose financial conditions are working their magic. Bloomberg: "Retail Sales Surge in Broad Advance, Topping Estimates." Retail Sales were up 0.6% (expectations 0.1%) for the month and 3.3% y-o-y. Empire Manufacturing and the Philadelphia Fed Business Outlook were much stronger-than-expected, with Industrial Production and Capacity Utilization above expectations. Housing Starts and Building Permits beat forecasts. University of Michigan Current Conditions gained two points to a stronger-than-expected 66.8, up nearly eight points in two months to the highest reading since January. For comparison, Current Con ditions were at 62.7% last July.

Inflation data, as it has lately, offered something for everyone. June headline CPI was reported at 0.3%, up from May 0.1% - placing y-o-y CPI at a four-month high of 2.7%. "Core" CPI added a tenth to a four-month high of 2.9%. Axios: "Trump's trade war begins to show up in inflation data." JPMorgan: "June 2025 CPI report: Tariffs are having an impact on consumer prices." NYT: "U.S. Inflation Accelerated in June as Trump's Tariffs Pushed Up Prices - Prices of products most exposed to tariffs, like household furnishings, jumped 1%, significantly higher than the 0.3 rise last month. Prices for appliances, specifically, rose 1.9%... The apparel index increased 0.4%, snapping multiple months of declining prices. Gasoline prices rose 1% in June… Grocery prices also rose, ticking up 0.3% in June."

July 15 - Wall Street Journal (Editorial Board): "President Trump insists there is 'no inflation' as he bludgeons the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates. But denying inflation reality won't make it go away. His tariffs are making it harder for the central bank to do what he wants, as last month's rise in the consumer-price index shows. The… consumer-price report… showed inflation ticked up in June… Price increases were broad-based, and especially in goods that the U.S. imports. Think toys (1.8%), paper products (1.4%) and appliances (1.9%). The latter was the biggest increase since August 2020. Food prices rose 0.3% despite a 7.4% decline in egg prices. One culprit was fresh fruit and vegetables (1%), the biggest increase in more than a year. About 60% of fresh fruit and 35% of fresh vegetables are imported."

As for tariffs, we're in uncharted territory with great uncertainty. Especially with inflation exceeding the Fed's 2% target for more than four years, it's uncentral banker-like to dismiss inflation risk.

To argue a 3% "neutral rate" and currently overly restrictive monetary policy is a stretch. After an abrupt early-April ("liberation day") tightening, financial conditions loosened dramatically throughout Q2. This was earnings week for the major "banks." And when it comes to financial conditions, it is always Credit and financial sector expansions where the analytical rubber meets the road. Before highlighting some Q2 bank data for the largest banks, it's important to appreciate the financial boom that had taken full control during Q1.

From Q1 Z.1 report analysis: Bank Assets jumped $581 billion, or 8.4% annualized, during Q1 to a record $28.388 TN - the largest quarterly growth since Q4 2021. Bank Assets ballooned $6.988 TN, or 33%, over 21 quarters. For Q1, Bank Loans increased $52 billion, or 1.4%, to a record $14.926 TN… Meanwhile, Repo Assets surged $84 billion, or 48% annualized (strongest since Q4 '18), to a record $782 billion - with one-year growth of $147 billion, or 23%. Bank Debt Securities holdings jumped $172 billion, or 11.2% annualized, to $6.342 TN. Debt Securities ballooned $1.660 TN, or 35.4%, over 21 quarters. Agency Securities holdings jumped $91 billion, or 11.9% annualized, with Corporate Bonds up $71 billion, or 31.9% annualized."

A few of the week's headlines: "US banking giants reap gains from dealmaking rebound, trading bump." "For Big Banks, the Trump Era Is Proving Profitable Thus Far." "JPMorgan Chase tops estimates on stronger-than-expected trading, investment banking." "JPMorgan Earnings Show Economy and Wall Street Are Still Chugging Along." "Goldman Sachs Profit Surges on Higher Trading Revenue." "Citigroup beats second-quarter estimates as markets and banking revenues jump." "Bank of America Reports Increase in Second-Quarter Profit."

July 15 - New York Times (Rob Copeland and Stacy Cowley): "For years, Wall Street's top bankers have watched with a mix of envy and exhaustion as power, profits and the popular imagination shifted westward to Silicon Valley. Being out of the spotlight, however, is proving profitable in the second Trump era. While many industries have been upended by the president's topsy-turvy trade and immigration policies, Wall Street is quietly humming along just fine. Some of the biggest lenders in America reported strong quarterly earnings on Tuesday along with increasing - if tentative - optimism that the U.S. economy has more room to run. The reports were a reminder that in the world of high finance, uncertainty is often a chance to make money."

July 16 - Bloomberg (Paul J. Davies): "Where are all of David Solomon's critics now? Those who haven't left Goldman Sachs Group Inc. for other firms are getting on with their jobs with gusto. The investment bank's chief executive officer had a torrid time cleaning up his strategic misstep into consumer banking, but two years after ending that project, its core businesses are on fire. Goldman reported record equity trading revenue in second-quarter earnings… and trounced its peers with a rebound in investment banking revenue that was fueled by a 70% jump in deal-making fees versus the same period last year… In equities, Goldman reported record revenue of $4.3 billion, which was made up of fees from cash and derivatives trading as clients kept adjusting portfolios to the ups and downs of White House policy, along with record lending to hedge funds, too. The result keeps it ahead of nearest rivals Morgan Stanley and JPMorgan…"

July 16 - Bloomberg (Hannah Levitt): "Morgan Stanley's stock traders scored their best second quarter on record as the biggest US banks continue to reap the benefits of market volatility tied to President Donald Trump's policy moves. The firm earned $3.72 billion in equity-trading revenue, a 23% jump from a year ago and ahead of analyst expectations… The firm's closely-watched wealth management unit reeled in $59.2 billion of net new assets in the period, also surpassing predictions."

July 16 - Financial Times (Akila Quinio): "Bank of America notched up better than expected profits in the second quarter as the US lender expanded its deposit base and loan book and benefited from robust Wall Street trading activity. The group's net income rose about 3% to $7.1bn, exceeding analyst expectations of $6.8bn. Revenues rose 4% to $26.5bn, slightly missing forecasts of $26.7bn. Revenues at the retail bank rose 6% to $10.8bn as the group grew its deposit base and loan book and benefited from higher net interest income…"

Bank earnings analysis is fine, but balance sheets are where the action is at. JPMorgan Total Assets surged $194.6 billion, or 18% annualized, to a record $4.552 TN during the quarter. This put first-half growth at a staggering $549.7 billion, or 27.5%. Total Loan growth accelerated to $56.3 billion, or 16.6% annualized - with first-half growth of $64.0 billion, or 9.5%. Total Commercial Loan growth accelerated to $43.8 billion, or 23.7% annualized, to a record $785 billion (1st-half $62.7bn, or 17.4% annualized). Total Consumer Loan growth accelerated to $12.5 billion, or 8.1% annualized, to a record $627 billion (1st-half $1.3bn, or 0.4% annualized).

"Fed Funds Sold & Repos" jumped $41.1 billion, or 38% annualized, to a record $470.6 billion - with first-half growth of $175.6 billion, or 120% annualized. Short-Term and Long-Term Investments surged $96.3 billion, or 26% annualized, to a record $1.575 TN, pushing first-half growth to $317 billion, or 50% annualized. Trading Securities posted first-half growth of $252.7 billion, or 88% annualized, to a record $829.5 billion.

"Other Assets" jumped $29.0 billion, or 39% annualized, to a $326 billion - with first-half growth of $63.3 billion, or 28% annualized.

On the Liability side, Total Deposits expanded $66.5 billion, or 10.7% annualized, to a record $2.562 TN - with first-half growth of $156.3 billion, or 13.0% annualized. "Short-Term Borrowings and Repos" surged $86.0 billion, or 46% annualized, to a record $833.9 billion - with notable first-half growth of $329.3 billion, or 130% annualized. Category detail will follow, but it's worth noting that "Securities Sold Under Repo" ballooned an unprecedented $180 billion during Q1 to a record $834 billion. "Trading Account Liabilities" surged $23.4 billion during Q2, or 63% annualized, to $173 billion.

Bank of America Total Asset growth of $91.7 billion, or 11% annualized, to a record $3.441 TN, pushed first-half growth to $179.6 billion, or 11% annualized. Total Loan growth accelerated to $35 billion, or 12.5% annualized, with first-half growth of $47.1 billion, or 8.5% annualized. Total Commercial Loan growth accelerated to $31.7 billion, or 19.7% annualized (to a record $674bn), with first-half growth of $43.4 billion, or 13.8% annualized. Total Consumer Loan growth increased to $4.8 billion, or 4.1% annualized (to $473bn), with first-half growth of $7.8 billion, or a 3.3% rate.

The asset "Fed Funds Sold & Repos" posted growth of $24 billion, or 29.3% annualized to $352 billion, with first-half growth of $77.7 billion, or 57% annualized.

On the Liability side, Total Deposits grew $22 billion, or 4.4% annualized, with first-half growth of $46.1 billion, or 4.7% annualized. "Short-Term Borrowings & Repos" surged $31.8 billion, or 24.3% annualized, to a record $555 billion - with first-half growth of $85.3 billion, or 36.3% annualized.

Citigroup Total Assets expanded $51.3 billion, or 8.0% annualized, during Q2 to a record $2.623 TN, boosting first-half growth to $269.8 billion, or 22.9%, annualized. Total Loans expanded $29.9 billion, or 15.7% annualized during Q2 and $44.0 billion, or 11.8% annualized, for the first half. Total Commercial Loans jumped $13.6 billion, or 17.2% annualized, with first-half growth of $28.4 billion, or 18.9% annualized. Total Consumer Loans expanded $9.7 billion, or 10.0% annualized during Q2 - and $2.4 billion, or 1.2% annualized, during the first half.

On the Liability side, Total Deposits surged $41.3 billion, or 12.6% annualized, during Q2, with first-half growth of $73.3 billion, or 11.4% annualized. "Short-Term Borrowings & Repos" expanded $27.8 billion, or 17.6% annualized, during the quarter - and $202.3 billion, or 89% annualized, during the first half. Q2 detail to follow, but "Securities Sold Under Repo" ballooned $146.5 billion during Q1 to $386 billion.

The NYSE Broker/Dealer Index (XBD) surged 3.8% this week, boosting 2025 returns to 29.8%. It is simply not credible to argue that monetary policy is restrictive or that the Fed should aggressively slash interest rates. The administration's attack on Powell is borderline despicable.

July 16 - Bloomberg (Lucia Mutikani): "President Donald Trump said he's not planning to fire Jerome Powell, and still managed to make it sound like a threat. Trump's comments capped a hectic few hours that took his pressure campaign against the Federal Reserve chief to a new level - and sent markets into a shortlived nosedive. He ran the idea of sacking Powell by a receptive group of Republican lawmakers late Tuesday. An aide said Wednesday morning he was likely to follow through. Then the president publicly backpedaled - with a major caveat. 'I don't rule out anything, but I think it's highly unlikely, unless he has to leave for fraud,' Trump said Wednesday when asked about axing the Fed chair."

July 17 - Associated Press (Christopher Rugaber): "Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell… said the agency's renovation of two of its buildings is in compliance with plans approved by a local commission, disputing a White House suggestion that they may have violated the law by deviating from those plans. The letter is the latest salvo in an escalating battle between the Federal Reserve, an independent agency charged with fighting inflation and seeking maximum employment, and the Trump administration… Vought said in his letter that if the renovation plans had changed, they were no longer 'in compliance with the approved plan' and may violate the National Capital Planning Act. Powell responded… that since the Fed's plans were approved by the NCPC in September 2021, it has made only 'a small number of design changes to scale back or eliminate certain elements' and added that the changes weren't significant enough to 'warrant... further review.' 'The project is proceeding in accordance with the plan that the NCPC approved,' Powell wrote. The changes were intended 'to simplify construction and reduce the likelihood of further delays and cost increases,' Powell said in his letter."

I refer to the "global government finance Bubble" for good reason. It's worth noting that China's state-directed banks increased total assets $1.915 TN during Q1 to a record $63.920 TN, the strongest growth in two years. This pushed one-year growth to over $4.0 TN, of 6.7%.

Quietly, China is pumping out record quantities of (globally impactful) Credit. Aggregate Financing, China's metric for system Credit, expanded a stronger-than-expected $586 billion during June, up from May's $319 billion and June '24's $460 billion. First half growth of $3.185 TN was up 26% from comparable 2024. Record one-year growth of $5.159 TN exceeds even pandemic 2020's $4.852 TN. Historic Credit expansion belies all the China deflation talk.

Corporate Loan growth of $246 billion (up 8.9% y-o-y) was up from May's $75 billion and June '24's $227 billion, with first-half growth of $1.608 TN up 5% from comparable 2024. Consumer Loans increased $83 billion (up 3.0% y-o-y), up from May's $8 billion and June '24's $80 billion. Government Bonds surged $188 billion for the month, easily surpassing June '24's $119 billion. Year-to-date growth of $1.067 TN more than doubled comparable 2024. Record one-year growth of $2.177 TN compares to pandemic 2020's $1.162 TN.

Vulnerable global bond markets will closely monitor Sunday's national elections in Japan.

July 15 - Wall Street Journal (Ronnie Harui): "Growing fears that Japan's upcoming parliamentary election will strain its fiscal position sent yields on long-dated government bonds sharply higher. Markets are fretting the Upper House election on July 20 could strip the ruling coalition of its majority and spur an increase in fiscal spending, one that might be financed by more bond issuance. The 20-year yield on Japanese government debt rose 4.5 bps to 2.650% early on Tuesday, its highest since November 1999… The 30-year yield rose 4.0 bps to a record high of 3.195%, while the 10-year yield rose 2.5 bps to 1.595%, the highest since October 2008. Market participants seem to be bracing for election scenarios that could have a major impact on the Japanese government bond market, such as consumption-tax cuts, Ataru Okumura, senior Japan rates strategist at SMBC Nikko Securities, said…"
